title: x/website: go.dev does not serve /.well-known/security.txt (RFC 9116) despite an advertised security policy
author: kobihikri
body:
### What is happening

`https://go.dev/.well-known/security.txt` returns **HTTP 404**, and so does `https://golang.org/.well-known/security.txt`.

Meanwhile Go advertises a formal security/vulnerability reporting process — `https://go.dev/security/policy` returns HTTP 200 and describes how to report — so there is a policy, just no machine-discoverable `security.txt` pointing to it.

### Why it matters

[RFC 9116](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9116) defines `/.well-known/security.txt` as the standard, machine-readable place a researcher (or automated tooling) looks first to find where and how to report a security issue. Serving one that points at the existing policy makes Go's already-published process discoverable by the convention researchers actually check.

### Suggested fix

Serve a small `security.txt` at `go.dev/.well-known/security.txt` (via the `x/website` site) with at least the RFC-mandatory fields, e.g.:

```
Contact: https://go.dev/security/policy
Expires: <a future date, per RFC 9116 §2.5.5>
Policy: https://go.dev/security/policy
```

I'm happy to send the change through Gerrit if this seems worthwhile.

### Reproduction

```
curl -sSL -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://go.dev/.well-known/security.txt   # => 404
curl -sSL -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://go.dev/security/policy            # => 200
```

I used AI assistance to help investigate and draft this; I reproduced the facts above myself and take responsibility for the report.

### What did you do?

Compile:
```go
package carry

import "math/bits"

func AddUseCarry(a, b [2]uint64, s uint64) uint64 {
	_, c := bits.Add64(a[0], b[0], 0)
	_, c2 := bits.Add64(a[1], b[1], c)
	return s + c2 // carry consumed by a plain add
}
```

### What did you see happen?

```
TEXT command-line-arguments.AddUseCarry(SB), ABIInternal
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.a(SP), CX
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.b+16(SP), DX
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.a+8(SP), BX
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.b+24(SP), SI
    ADDQ CX, DX
    ADCQ SI, BX
    ADCQ $0, AX
    RET
```

### What did you expect to see?

```
TEXT command-line-arguments.AddUseCarry(SB), ABIInternal
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.b+16(SP), DX
    MOVQ command-line-arguments.a+8(SP), BX
    ADDQ command-line-arguments.a(SP), DX
    ADCQ command-line-arguments.b+24(SP), BX
    ADCQ $0, AX
    RET
```

title: cmd/compile: materialized bits.Add64/Sub64 carries no longer fold into consumers on amd64 (regression from CL 778140)
author: gaul
body:
### Go version

`go version go1.27-devel_63d836396a linux/amd64` (regression vs `go version go1.26.5 linux/amd64`)

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace

```
GOARCH=amd64
GOOS=linux
GOAMD64=v1
```

(other fields defaults; not relevant)

### What did you do?

Compiled a `bits.Add64` chain whose final carry is consumed by ordinary arithmetic, the pattern that fiat-crypto generated code (`crypto/internal/fips140/nistec/fiat`, `edwards25519`, `mldsa`) hits pervasively via `p521Uint1(x) + y`:

```go
package carry

import "math/bits"

func AddUseCarry(a, b [2]uint64, s uint64) uint64 {
	_, c := bits.Add64(a[0], b[0], 0)
	_, c2 := bits.Add64(a[1], b[1], c)
	return s + c2 // carry consumed by a plain add
}
```

`go build -gcflags=-S` with both toolchains.

### What did you see happen?

go1.26.5 materializes and consumes the carry in two instructions — `SBBQ` produces a 0/−1 carrymask and the negation folds into the consumer (`ADDQ x (NEGQ mask)` → `SUBQ x mask`):

```
ADDQ  CX, DX
ADCQ  SI, BX
SBBQ  CX, CX
SUBQ  CX, AX
```

Tip (63d836396a) spends three, because `MOVBQZX (SETB flags)` participates in no folds:

```
ADDQ    CX, DX
ADCQ    SI, BX
SETCS   CL
MOVBLZX CL, CX
ADDQ    CX, AX
```

This comes from commit 212065c922 ([CL 778140](https://go.dev/cl/778140), "cmd/compile: shuffle bits.Sub intrinsic generation on amd64", updates #76056), which changed the lowering of `Select1 (Add64carry/Sub64borrow)` from `NEGQ (SBBQcarrymask flags)` to `MOVBQZX (SETB flags)` so the carry is easier to pattern-match. For carries that feed the next `Add64carry`/`Sub64borrow` directly nothing changes — the round-trip elision was carried over (`(Select1 (NEGLflags (MOVBQZX (SETB x)))) => x`, AMD64.rules line 44) — and for a carry that is only returned the two forms tie at two instructions. But a carry consumed by arithmetic lost the `(ADDQ x (NEGQ y)) => (SUBQ x y)` composition, costing one instruction and 3 bytes per site.

fiat-generated field arithmetic consumes carries this way constantly. Copying the (byte-identical between the two releases) `p521_fiat64.go` into a standalone module and building its test binary with both toolchains:

| function | | go1.26.5 | tip 63d836396a | delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `p521Mul` | text bytes | 9164 | 9310 | **+146** |
| | instructions | 2399 | 2425 | +26 |
| | setcc/movzx | 0 / 0 | 36 / 36 | |
| | adc/sbb | 324 | 288 | −36 |
| `p521Square` | text bytes | 7621 | 7821 | **+200** |
| | instructions | 2007 | 2048 | +41 |
| | setcc/movzx | 0 / 0 | 36 / 36 | |
| | adc/sbb | 324 | 288 | −36 |

(The 36 dropped `sbb`s are the former carrymask materializations, now `setcc`; in 1.26.5 twenty-six of their negations folded into consumer `SUBQ`s and ten remained as `NEGQ`, i.e. 46 instructions total vs 72 now.)

Benchmarking the same standalone module (AMD Ryzen AI MAX+ 395, linux/amd64, count=5, spread < 1%):

```
            go1.26.5   tip 63d836396a
P521Mul     58.4 ns    59.8 ns   (+2.4%)
P521Square  49.7 ns    51.2 ns   (+2.9%)
```

An instruction-pattern scan (x86lint) of the fips140 packages linked into cmd/go counts ~520 new adjacent `setcc; movzx` pairs from this lowering (nistec/fiat 279, mldsa 86, nistec 75, edwards25519 46, bigmod 19).

### What did you expect to see?

At least parity with go1.26.5. [CL 778140](https://go.dev/cl/778140)'s commit message describes the change as a no-op, which holds for isolated carries but not for consumed ones.

The `SETB` canonicalization itself seems like a good direction for #76056; what is missing is folds that let consumers eat the materialized form, e.g.

```
(ADDQ x (MOVBQZX (SETB f))) => consume CF directly (ADCQconst [0] x f — one instruction, better than either release)
```

and analogous ADDQconst/SUBQ forms, falling back to something no worse than the old `SBBQ`+`SUBQ` pair when the flags value cannot be scheduled to the consumer. With those in place this pattern would beat go1.26.5 rather than trail it.

cc @Jorropo @randall77

I am trying to build the ivy example for mobile on iOS, and failing very early. There are a number of issues, but the first I hit is that the default iOS version is 13.0, which is rejected by XCode running on my up-to-date mac.

Following the readme:

M	go.sum

bismarck=% gomobile bind -target=ios,iossimulator,maccatalyst,macos robpike.io/ivy/mobile robpike.io/ivy/demo

<arget=ios,iossimulator,maccatalyst,macos robpike.io                         /ivy/mobile robpike.io/iv
<catalyst,macos robpike.io/ivy/mobile robpike.io/ivy                         /demo
gomobile: maccatalyst/amd64: go build -buildmode=c-archive -o /var/folders/hs/fxh5g1g977x6bvfxsk5vc27w0000gn/T/gomobile-work-2725062086/mobile-maccatalyst-amd64.a ./gobind failed: exit status 1
# runtime/cgo
clang: error: invalid version number in '-target x86_64-apple-ios13.0-macabi'

bismarck=% 

The last line is the issue.

In general, the x/mobile repo needs a little touchup by someone who understands how it works.

### What did you see happen?

Failure to build.

### What did you expect to see?

Build success. This exact recipe worked fine a few months ago. I think it needs a little modernization, thanks to Apple's intense dislike for backwards compatibility.

title: proposal: go/doc: indicate subheadings by prefixing with `## ` and `### `
author: sfllaw
body:
## Background In https://golang.org/cl/5437056, we added the concept of headings. And in https://github.com/golang/go/discussions/48305, we decided to add a Markdown-like prefix to indicate these headings: `# `. See https://go.dev/doc/comment#headings. I have noticed that people keep accidentally writing unsupported subheadings in their documentation comments: `## ` and `### `. Here are some examples: - [cmd/compile/internal/noder](https://pkg.go.dev/cmd/compile/internal/noder), although doc.go is malformatted so its documentation is hidden ([#80397](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/80397)). - [golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/inline](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/inline) In the wild, Sourcegraph finds so many repositories that do this: [`context:global lang:Go /^\/\/ ## \w/ count:100000`](https://sourcegraph.com/search?q=context:global+lang:Go+/%5E%5C/%5C/+%23%23+%5Cw/+count:100000&patternType=keyword&sm=0). Note that some of these are false positives, but it’s obvious that people habitually use this convention without realizing that it doesn’t work. ## Proposal Details I propose that we add `## ` and `### ` prefixes and make them denote subheadings and subsubheadings. https://go.dev/doc/comment#headings should be changed to read: > A heading is a line beginning with at least one number sign (U+0023) and then a space and the heading text. It should also elaborate on subheadings and subsubheadings, including worked examples. ### HTML for `go doc -http` **Currently:** - `# Title` is rendered as `

Title

` **Proposed:** - `## Subtitle` is rendered as `
Subtitle
` - `### Subsubtitle` is rendered as `
Subsubtitle
` We shouldn’t support `#### ` because that would require the non-standard `` tag.

title: runtime: consider removing randomizedheapbase64 GOEXPERIMENT opt-out
author: rolandshoemaker
body:
As far as I'm aware we haven't hit any particular issues with the randomizedheapbase64 GOEXPERIMENT, which has been enabled by default since 1.26. It does make some debugging tools behave a little funky, but I believe most have figured out ways around it.

We should consider removing the GOEXPERIMENT, which at this point exists solely to let users opt-out of this behavior.

title: runtime: use process_madvise to batch scavenger memory releases on Linux

title: x/pkgsite: evaluation page
author: jba
body:
Add a page for each package that shows evaluations for that package. 

An evaluation is a signal, a heuristic for package or module quality. We show some on the main page: whether the version is tagged and stable, for example. We would like to show others, and the main page isn't big enough.

title: x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize: errorsastype panics on package-level errors.As target var
author: otherview
body:
### Go version

go version go1.26.5 darwin/arm64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='clang'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='clang++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOARCH='arm64'
GOARM64='v8.0'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/Users/pedro/Library/Caches/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/Users/pedro/Library/Application Support/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOHOSTARCH='arm64'
GOHOSTOS='darwin'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/Users/pedro/go/src/github.com/vechain/thor/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/Users/pedro/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='darwin'
GOPATH='/Users/pedro/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/Users/pedro/go/pkg/mod/golang.org/toolchain@v0.0.1-go1.26.5.darwin-arm64'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/Users/pedro/Library/Application Support/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/Users/pedro/go/pkg/mod/golang.org/toolchain@v0.0.1-go1.26.5.darwin-arm64/pkg/tool/darwin_arm64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.26.5'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

The `errorsastype` modernizer panics when the variable passed to `errors.As`
is declared at **package scope** rather than as a local `var` statement.

Minimal, runnable reproducer — `main.go`:

```go
package main

import (
      "errors"
      "fmt"
      "io/fs"
)

// package-level declaration of the errors.As target
var target *fs.PathError

func check(err error) {
      if errors.As(err, &target) {
              fmt.Println("path error")
      }
}

func main() { check(nil) }
```

Run the modernize analyzer over it:

```
go run golang.org/x/tools/gopls/internal/analysis/modernize/cmd/modernize@v0.23.0 ./...
```

```

Reproduces with gopls v0.22.0 and v0.23.0; v0.21.1 and earlier are unaffected
(they predate the errorsastype pass). Moving the declaration inside the
function (a local `var target *fs.PathError` immediately before the `if`) makes
the panic go away.
```
### What did you see happen?

The analyzer panics instead of producing a diagnostic:

```
panic: interface conversion: *ast.File is not ast.Stmt: missing method stmtNode

goroutine 5 [running]:
golang.org/x/tools/internal/refactor.DeleteStmt(...)
      .../golang.org/x/tools/internal/refactor/delete.go:382
golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize.errorsastype-range1(...)
      .../golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize/errorsastype.go:127
golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize.errorsastype(...)
      .../golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/modernize/errorsastype.go:74
golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/checker.(*Action).execOnce(...)
      .../golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/checker/checker.go:378
...
exit status 2
```

Root cause: `canUseErrorsAsType` returns `curDecl.Parent()` as the declaration
statement (errorsastype.go:242), with the comment "curDecl.Parent() is a
DeclStmt". That holds only for a *local* `var`. For a package-level `var`, the
`GenDecl`'s parent is the `*ast.File`, not an `*ast.DeclStmt`. That cursor is
passed to `refactor.DeleteStmt` (errorsastype.go:127), whose first line asserts
`curStmt.Node().(ast.Stmt)` (delete.go:382); `*ast.File` doesn't implement
`ast.Stmt`, so it panics.

### What did you expect to see?

Either a valid `errors.As` → `errors.AsType` suggestion, or the pass safely
skipping this case with no diagnostic. A malformed AST / package-scoped target
should never crash the analyzer.

A minimal fix is to guard in `canUseErrorsAsType` that the declaration is a
local statement before returning it, e.g. reject when
`curDecl.Parent().Node()` is not an `*ast.DeclStmt` (package-level vars, whose
parent is `*ast.File`).

```
This is a distinct bug from the (fixed) #80031 — that was an invalid rewrite when
the target type doesn't implement error; this is a panic (interface conversion:
*ast.File is not ast.Stmt) when the errors.As target var is package-scoped. The
#80031 fix (the types.AssignableTo error-check) is present in the build tested
here and does not cover this path. Related: #75692 (original errorsastype pass).
```

title: proposal: x/crypto/acme/autocert: enable ECDSA P-384 support for the CNSA 2.0 compatibility
author: vsukhoml
body:
### Proposal Details

Currently `autocert` hardcodes request of ECDSA P-256 certificate from the Let's encrypt. While this well-supported curve, it is not in the CNSA 2.0, which requires either ML-DSA or ECDSA P384. P384 curve is supported by the Let's encrypt, so it can be a trivial upgrade enabling more use cases  out of the box.

title: proposal: text/template, html/template: add ExecuteContext and ExecuteTemplateContext
author: binwiederhier
body:
### Proposal Details

I propose adding context-aware execution methods to `text/template` and `html/template`, so a long-running render can be stopped by a deadline or a `CancelFunc`. On `text/template.Template`:

```go
// ExecuteContext is like [Template.Execute], but stops execution and returns
// ctx.Err() (either [context.Canceled] or [context.DeadlineExceeded]) if ctx is
// canceled or its deadline is exceeded before the template finishes rendering.
func (t *Template) ExecuteContext(ctx context.Context, wr io.Writer, data any) error

// ExecuteTemplateContext is like [Template.ExecuteTemplate], but stops execution
// and returns ctx.Err() if ctx is canceled or its deadline is exceeded.
func (t *Template) ExecuteTemplateContext(ctx context.Context, wr io.Writer, name string, data any) error
```

`html/template.Template` gets the same two methods, forwarding to the escaped text template the way `Execute` already does. `Execute` and `ExecuteTemplate` become `ExecuteContext(context.Background(), ...)` wrappers, so existing callers are unchanged.

Cancellation is checked between template operations, so a blocking or CPU-bound template function is not interrupted until it returns. On cancellation the methods return `ctx.Err()` directly, not wrapped in `ExecError` (consistent with how write errors are already returned unwrapped today).

### Motivation

Template execution cannot be interrupted: `Execute` runs to completion. An untrusted or generated template with a tight or nested `{{range}}` can run essentially unbounded. This example needs no input data and is a single `Execute` call of a billion node evaluations that writes nothing, so it never touches the output writer:

```
{{range 1000}}{{range 1000}}{{range 1000}}{{end}}{{end}}{{end}}
```

There is no way to stop that from outside the package. The executor's per-node `state.walk` loop is unexported, so a caller cannot reach into it. Closing the output writer from another goroutine only takes effect on the next `Write`, which never comes for a no-output loop (and `io.Writer.Close` is not documented as safe to call concurrently with `Write`). `{{return}}` (#53015) is cooperative and in-template, so it cannot express a caller-side deadline.

This is not hypothetical for me. I maintain [ntfy](github.com/binwiederhier/ntfy), which lets users send a Go template that is rendered against a JSON body. I received two independent security advisories and a Discord report of this exact denial of service. ntfy already guarded template execution with a timeout that closed the output writer, and it was still exploitable, because the reported payload wrote no output and so never hit a `Write`. The only fix I could find was to vendor `text/template` and move the interrupt into `walk` ([PR #1826](https://github.com/binwiederhier/ntfy/pull/1826), vendored at [template/gotext](https://github.com/binwiederhier/ntfy/tree/main/template/gotext). `ExecuteContext` would let ntfy, and anyone else who has forked the package for this, drop the fork.

### Prior art

#31107 proposed the same two methods back in 2019, but bundled a second feature:
passing per-execution values (CSP nonces, CSRF tokens) into templates. The discussion centered on that half, and it was declined for lack of consensus; cancellation itself was not the objection. This proposal drops the value passing and asks only for cancellation. I could not find another open proposal for it. #36462 (revise the execution model) and #53015 (`{{return}}`) are adjacent, but neither gives a caller a way to stop a running render.

### Possible implementation

The change is small and additive, and a non-cancelable context does no extra work, so the existing `Execute` path is unchanged; In my PoC, I measured no difference against it on a worst-case render. The exact mechanism is not part of the proposed API, and reviewers may prefer a different one.

I have this implemented, with tests (cancel by deadline, cancel by `CancelFunc`, a no-output loop, and cancellation propagating into nested templates). I will hold off on an official CL until it is wanted; in the meantime, here is a working proof of concept: https://github.com/binwiederhier/go/pull/1

title: proposal: cmd/go: add GOVCSROOT to declare repo roots for private modules without HTTP discovery
author: mendymm
body:
### Proposal Details

Add a `GOVCSROOT` environment variable pointing to a file that maps import path prefixes to repos, using the `go-import` meta tag syntax, so private modules can be fetched without HTTP discovery.

## Problem

`go get` currently fails for private GitLab repos that sit under subgroups and are only reachable over SSH. The root cause is in how `go` resolves an import path to a repo.

Given an import path, `go` needs to figure out which VCS repo it belongs to. It tries, in order:

1. Known-host regexes (GitHub, Bitbucket, etc.), fixed depth.
2. Generic `.git`/`.hg`/etc. suffix fallback, any host.
3. `?go-get=1` discovery, if both above fail to match.

1 and 3 can't handle private GitLab repos with subgroups: the repo root can sit at any depth, and neither a fixed regex nor an unauthenticated discovery fetch can determine that from the URL alone. 2 does work, but only by permanently baking `.git` into the canonical import path, which teams standardizing on clean import paths reject.

Once the repo URL is known, `go` delegates cloning to the underlying VCS tool, which handles its own auth.

Discovery is the one step that isn't delegated this way: the `?go-get=1` fetch is made by `go`'s own HTTP client, with no access to whatever auth git/ssh already has configured. For a GitLab subgroup repo reachable only over SSH, this fetch has no HTTPS credentials to use, so `go` ends up with a truncated repo root, e.g. `gitlab.com/acme/group.git`, which doesn't exist. With the usual gitconfig `insteadOf` rewrite to SSH in place, the clone then fails with:

```
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.
Please make sure you have the correct access rights and the repository exists.
```

### Why GOAUTH (Go 1.24) doesn't help here

`GOAUTH` can authenticate the discovery fetch, but only with HTTP credentials. On a machine that has nothing but an SSH key, there are no credentials for it to supply, so discovery still fails. It also has no bearing on the clone itself: that's the VCS tool talking over SSH, outside `GOAUTH` entirely.


### Existing workarounds and their limits

| Workaround | Limitation |
|---|---|
| `.netrc` + gitconfig `insteadOf` | Requires HTTPS credentials, unusable in SSH-only setups |
| `replace` directive in `go.mod` | Pins a version manually; `go get -u` won't discover updates for it, and the directive is ignored by downstream consumers |
| `.git` suffix in import path | Permanently changes the canonical import path |
| Private GOPROXY (Athens, etc.) | Significant infra overhead for small teams/individuals |

## Proposal

Introduce a `GOVCSROOT` environment variable pointing to a plain text file that maps import path prefixes to repos, bypassing discovery entirely. Cloning and auth stay exactly as delegated to the VCS tool as they are today; `GOVCSROOT` only removes the one step that isn't.

- Default location: `os.UserConfigDir()/go/vcsroot`, same pattern as `GOENV`.
- File format: one mapping per line, using the same fields as a `go-import` meta tag's `content` attribute, space-separated: `root-path vcs repo-url [subdirectory]`. `#` comments, blank lines ignored.
  - `root-path`: the import path prefix being declared. Matching is by path prefix; when multiple entries match, the longest prefix wins.
  - `vcs`: `git`, `hg`, `svn`, `bzr`, or `fossil` (the meta tag's `mod` value isn't valid here, since this mechanism exists specifically to bypass proxy/HTTP resolution).
  - `repo-url`: the clone URL, including scheme.
  - `subdirectory` (optional): as of Go 1.25, `go-import` meta tags can declare that `root-path` lives in a subdirectory of the repo rather than at its root. `GOVCSROOT` supports the same optional field, for the same reason (monorepos where the VCS root sits one level up from the import-path prefix).
- `GOPRIVATE` is still needed to bypass the module proxy and checksum DB; `GOVCSROOT` only handles discovery.

This reuses the `go-import` meta tag's own syntax as-is.

Example:
```
gitlab.com/acme/group/subgroup/repo git ssh://git@gitlab.com/acme/group/subgroup/repo.git
```

<details>
<summary>Prior art / related issues</summary>

| Issue | Summary | Status | Relationship |
|---|---|---|---|
| [#45611](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/45611) | Extend `GOPRIVATE` with VCS type, transport, credentials | Open, Hold | Closest prior proposal. Overloads `GOPRIVATE` with auth concerns (`rsc` objected). This proposal separates discovery from auth. |
| [#40189](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/40189) | `go auth` subcommand / `GOAUTH` | Closed, declined for [#26232](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/26232) | Auth problem, orthogonal to this proposal. |
| [#39536](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/39536) | `source` directive in `go.mod` mapping module to repo URL | Open, Hold | Per-module; `bcmills` noted N repos means N squared directives. This proposal is global, per-repo. |
| [#38964](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/38964) | `GOVCSEXT` for extra VCS paths/regex | Open, Hold | About selecting VCS type per host, not repo-root depth ambiguity. |
| [#26232](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/26232) | HTTP auth extension mechanism (`GOAUTH`) | Closed, Accepted, Go 1.24 | Solves the HTTPS credential problem, not SSH-only setups. |
| [#26134](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/26134) | Private repos on hardcoded hosting sites need special config | Open | `krak3n`'s comment describes this exact GitLab subgroup problem. |
| [#22640](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/22640) | `go get` fails when repo URL deeper than 2 levels | Closed, frozen | Original 2017 bug report for this problem, closed without a fix. |
| [#34094](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/34094) | GitLab subgroups: `go-import` meta tags refer to wrong repo | Closed | GitLab returning incorrect meta tags for unauthenticated subgroup requests. |
| [#42632](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/42632) | Cannot `go get` private GitLab repo in a subgroup | Closed, WaitingForInfo (stale) | Same symptom as [#34094](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/34094), reported independently. Corroborates that this is a recurring unresolved pain point. |

</details>

Happy to implement this if the proposal is accepted.

title: cmd/compile: improve mono.go from O(n^2) to O(n)
author: tom93
body:
### Proposal Details

*(This is more of a "suggestion" or "request for comments" than a proposal.)*

The current check for unbounded recursive instantiation in [src/cmd/compile/internal/types2/mono.go](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.26.5/src/cmd/compile/internal/types2/mono.go) uses an O(n^2) algorithm based on Bellman-Ford to find positive-weight cycles in a directed graph. There is a simple O(n) algorithm based on strongly-connected components (SCCs). Are you interested in switching to this algorithm?

Here is a contrived example that triggers the O(n^2) behaviour:

```go
package main
func main() {}
type Unbounded[T any] struct{ x *Unbounded[Unbounded[T]] }
type S1[T any] struct{ s *S1[T] }
type S2[T any] struct{ s *S2[T] }
...
type S100000[T any] struct{ s *S100000[T] }
```

The repetitive part can be generated in bash using

```sh
for ((i=1;i<=100000;i++)); do echo "type S$i[T any] struct{ s *S$i[T] }"; done
```

With go1.26.3, it takes 30 seconds for the compiler to report the error. Obviously it's contrived. It looks like the precise worst-case time complexity is O(V\*E) where V = total number of type parameters and E = total number of type arguments (roughly)* in the current package (across all generic type/function/method declarations); so I think the likelihood of this affecting people is fairly low, but the fast algorithm is pretty simple.

The idea is to use [Tarjan's algorithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tarjan's_strongly_connected_components_algorithm) or similar to identify SCCs in O(n), and then for each positive-weight edge A-->B check whether A and B belong to the same SCC; if so, that edge is part of a positive-weight cycle.

(This algorithm can be further improved by doing these checks during the execution of Tarjan's algorithm, since after Tarjan's algorithm processes an edge we can tell whether or not that edge is part of an SCC. Some further modifications allow reconstructing the cycle containing the edge.)

<!--
Aside: Here is an example that compiles successfully and also takes very long.

`n=100000; echo "package main"; echo "func main() {}"; echo "type S0[T any] struct{}"; echo "type X[T any] struct{s *S$n[[]T]}"; for ((i=1;i<=n;i++)); do echo "type S$i[T any] struct{ s *S$((i-1))[T] }"; done`
-->

---

<sup>*More precisely, it looks like E is the number of references to type parameters that occur in type arguments of instantiations of types/functions/methods defined in the current package.</sup>

title: runtime: crash after shrinkstack crash with cgo
author: codecor-cn
body:
### Go version

go version go1.26.4 (Red Hat 1.26.4-1.el10_2) linux/amd64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'

GOHOSTARCH='amd64'

GOINSECURE=''

GONOSUMDB=''

GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='local'
GOTOOLDIR='/usr/lib/golang/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.26.4 (Red Hat 1.26.4-1.el10_2)'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

We build a Go application which also include some CGO requests and very frequently we see errors produced by an unwinding crash. Unfortunately, we are not able to reproduce the crash but the crashes occurred often without executing any specific request to produce this error. 
we use cgo call c++ lib repo is https://github.com/pseudocodes/go2ctp at 6.7.13 branch
go mod is 1.25.9


### What did you see happen?

The program experiences two types of crashes. AI analysis reveals that both crashes stem from the same root cause: after stack contraction, the stack space fails to replicate smoothly, leading to incorrect handling of certain stack frames. This is evident from the fact that the stack sizes in both crash logs have become 4kb, whereas the initial stack size for Go language goroutines is 8kb. Furthermore, after utilizing the GODEBUG=gcshrinkstackoff=1 environment variable, the program runing not crash

The first crash log:
```
0xc6a2b881000 - 0xc6a2b880000 = 0x1000 = 4KB

runtime: g 10: unexpected return pc for xxxxxxxxxxxxxx called from 0xc6a2bbbbb20
stack: frame={sp:0xc6a2b880db0, fp:0xc6a2b880fc8} stack=[0xc6a2b880000,0xc6a2b881000)
fatal error: unknown caller pc

goroutine 10 gp=0xc6a2b8b4960 m=10 mp=0xc6a2ce80008 [running]:
runtime.systemstack_switch()
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/asm_amd64.s:516 +0x8 fp=0xc6a2b880c40 sp=0xc6a2b880c30 pc=0x48ef28
runtime.(*_panic).nextFrame(0x47060b?)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/panic.go:1003 +0x58 fp=0xc6a2b880c80 sp=0xc6a2b880c40 pc=0x450318
runtime.(*_panic).start(0x8?, 0x0?, 0x4170e4?)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/panic.go:924 +0x145 fp=0xc6a2b880ca0 sp=0xc6a2b880c80 pc=0x450125
panic({0xa3e600?, 0x11026d0?})
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/panic.go:854 +0x130 fp=0xc6a2b880d50 sp=0xc6a2b880ca0 pc=0x488f70
runtime.panicmem(...)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/panic.go:336
runtime.sigpanic()
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/signal_unix.go:931 +0x378 fp=0xc6a2b880db0 sp=0xc6a2b880d50 pc=0x48b5d8
xxxxxxxxxxxxxx(0xc6a2b881db0)
	/home/shiyu/code/yyyyyy:203 +0x245 fp=0xc6a2b880fc8 sp=0xc6a2b880db0 pc=0x586d45
runtime: g 10: unexpected return pc for xxxxxxxxxxxxxx called from 0xc6a2bbbbb20
stack: frame={sp:0xc6a2b880db0, fp:0xc6a2b880fc8} stack=[0xc6a2b880000,0xc6a2b881000)
```

The second crash log:
```
0x2f2fc170000 - 0x2f2fc16f000 = 0x1000 = 4KB

runtime: g56: frame.sp=0x2f2fc16feb0 top=0x2f2fc16ffe0
	stack=[0x2f2fc16f000-0x2f2fc170000
fatal error: traceback did not unwind completely

goroutine 56 gp=0x2f2efff2b40 m=nil [chan receive (scan), 72 minutes]:
runtime.gopark(0x0?, 0x2f2ed400220?, 0xf?, 0x0?, 0x2f2fb890c96?)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/proc.go:462 +0xce fp=0x2f2fc16fe10 sp=0x2f2fc16fdf0 pc=0x4894ce
runtime.chanrecv(0x2f2ed498380, 0x2f2fc16ffb0, 0x1)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/chan.go:667 +0x4ae fp=0x2f2fc16fe88 sp=0x2f2fc16fe10 pc=0x41e2ae
runtime.chanrecv2(0xb47c96?, 0x6?)
	/usr/lib/golang/src/runtime/chan.go:514 +0x12 fp=0x2f2fc16feb0 sp=0x2f2fc16fe88 pc=0x41ddf2
oooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo(0x0)
	/home/shiyu/code/iiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiii:275 +0x96 fp=0x2f2fc16ffc8 sp=0x2f2fc16feb0 pc=0x587396
created by pppppppppppppppppppp in goroutine 1
	/home/shiyu/code/jjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjj:69 +0x4c5
```

### What did you expect to see?

Can this crash be confirmed as an bug?
After the fix, can I operate normally without using GODEBUG=gcshrinkstackoff=1 environment variables
